1: The messenger formula haddavar asher haya el-Yirmeyahu meyet YHWH lemor opens the oracle unit. The formula marks a major new oracle section beginning with ch18; the potter's house sign-act (chs 18-19) forms a paired sequence with wet clay (malleable, ch18) and fired flask (irreparable, ch19).
2: Qum weyaradta bet hayotser — get up and go down to the potter's house. The command uses two verbs (qûm, arise; yārad, go down) — Anathoth being at higher elevation, a potter's workshop in the lower city would involve an actual descent. Weshamma ashmia et-devaray — and there I will make you hear my words: YHWH will use the visual scene to deliver the oracle — the lived observation precedes the verbal interpretation, a pattern common to Jeremiah's sign-acts.
3: Waered beit hayotser wehineh-hu oseh melacha al-ha-obnayim — I went down to the potter's house and there he was, working at the wheel. Hāʾobnayim (the two stones) is the technical term for the potter's wheel — a lower stone turned by the feet driving an upper stone on which the clay rested; the dual form captures the two-disc mechanism. The observation is matter-of-fact reportage, grounding the sign-act in observable physical reality before the oracle provides its interpretation.
4: Venishhat hakli asher hu oseh beyad hachomet — the clay vessel he was shaping was ruined in the potter's hand. Šāḥat (Niphal, be ruined/marred) — the clay did not respond properly; something in the clay itself resisted the forming. Wayashov wayaasehu keli acher kaasher yashar beinei hayotser laasot — so he reworked it into another vessel, as seemed right in the potter's eyes. The key phrase is kaʾăšer yāšar bĕʿênê hayyôṣēr — according to what was right in the potter's sight, not the clay's. The potter's sovereign judgment about what the clay should become is the theological point the following oracle will develop.
5: The interpretive oracle frame: wayehi devar-YHWH elay lemor — the word of YHWH came to me. The sign-act observation (vv3-4) is complete; the oracle follows as YHWH's authoritative interpretation of what Jeremiah saw.
6: Hacheyinner hayotser hazzeh lo uchal laasot lachem beit Yisrael — can I not deal with you as this potter has done with clay, O house of Israel? The rhetorical question (hăkĕ- prefix expecting a yes-answer) establishes the analogy. The key theological move: the potter does not destroy flawed clay, he reshapes it. YHWH as potter is not asserting a right to destroy Israel capriciously but a capacity to reshape them according to his sovereign purpose. Ke-chomer beyad hayotser ken atem beyadi beit Yisrael — like clay in the potter's hand, so you are in my hand. The simile is exact: the relationship of total sovereign control with capacity for remedial reshaping.
7: Reˁga adabber ˁal-goy veˁal-mamlakhah liˁtosh velintotz veliˁabed — at one moment I may speak concerning a nation or kingdom, to uproot, tear down, and destroy it. The three verbs (nāṯaš, nātaṣ, ʾāḇad) are three of the six commission-verbs of 1:10 — Jeremiah's call is now being given its operational framework: the announced destruction is not inevitable.
8: Veshav hagoy hahu meraˁato asher dibarti ˁalav venichamti ˁal haraˁa asher hashavti laˁasot lo — but if that nation turns (šāb) from its evil about which I spoke, I will relent (wĕniḥamtî) concerning the disaster I intended to bring on it. The verb nāḥam (Niphal, relent/repent/be comforted) is applied to YHWH — YHWH genuinely responds to human response. The Niphal is the reflexive: YHWH turns himself from the announced decree. This is the theological principle that undergirds the entire book's call to repentance.
9: Veraˁga adabber ˁal-goy veˁal-mamlakhah livnot velintoˁa — and at another moment I may declare concerning a nation or kingdom that I will build and plant it. The remaining two commission-verbs of 1:10 (bānâ, nāṭaʿ) are now made conditional — they too can be revoked.
10: Veˁasa haraˁa beˁeinai leshemoˁa beqoli venichamti ˁal hatova asher amarti lehitiv oto — but if it does evil in my eyes, not listening to my voice, I will relent of the good I said I would do for it. The symmetry is complete: just as announced judgment can be averted by repentance, announced blessing can be withdrawn by apostasy. This is not divine fickleness but divine responsiveness — YHWH's decrees are conditional on human covenantal fidelity. The principle becomes operative immediately in v11 for Judah.
11: Veˁatta emor-na el-ish Yehuda veˁal yoshve Yerushalayim lemor — and now speak to the men of Judah and inhabitants of Jerusalem. Hineni yotser aleichem raˁa — I am forming (yōṣēr, the potter-participle) disaster against you. YHWH is the active potter shaping the disaster just as the Jerusalem potter shaped his clay — the present participle (yōṣēr) signals ongoing action, not completed decision. Ushvu na ish midarkam haraˁa veheitivu darkechem umaˁalleichem — turn back each from his evil way and amend your ways and your deeds. The call to šûb (return/repent) is both collective and individual (ʾîš, each man).
12: Veamru noash ki-acharei machshevotenu nelech — but they say, It is hopeless (nôʾāš)! We will follow our own plans. Nôʾāš is the same word of resigned despair used in 2:25 — the people have moved beyond the feeling of hopelessness to the deliberate choice of it. Veish sherirut libo haraˁa naˁaseh — and each one will act according to the stubbornness of his evil heart (šĕrîrût libbô hārāˁâ). The phrase šĕrîrût lēḇ (stubbornness of heart) is Jeremiah's recurring term for the incorrigibility that makes repentance impossible (cf. 3:17; 7:24; 9:14; 11:8; 16:12).
13: Lachen koh amar YHWH shellu-na bagoyim — therefore thus says YHWH, ask among the nations. The divine appeal to the nations as external witness (cf. 2:10-11) returns: Israel's behavior is so anomalous that even the pagan nations would find it incredible. Naˁarat Yisrael ˁasta shaˁaruriya meod — the virgin Israel has done a truly horrifying thing (šaʿărûrît, a thing that causes the hair to stand on end, from śāˁar, hair/storm).
14: Hayaˁazov misheleg Levanon tsur hassade — does the snow of Lebanon leave the mountain crags? The two natural phenomena — Lebanon's permanent snow and the cold mountain streams — are used as standards of constancy to indict Israel's inconstancy. Ayin tsinnurim mayim zarim kefuyim — do the cold flowing waters from afar run dry? The cold mountain streams flowing continuously from distant heights are reliable precisely because they are fed by a source they did not create. Israel had such a source (YHWH, the spring of living water, 2:13) and abandoned it.
15: Ki shachechuni ˁammi lashav yaqteru — for my people have forgotten me; they burn offerings to worthless things (lāšāwĕʾ, to vanity/emptiness). Wayikashlu bedarcheyhem shevilot ˁolam — they have stumbled from the ancient paths (šĕḇîlôt ʿôlām, the age-old trails). The ancient paths are the established covenant way; departing from them is not progress but stumbling — the directional metaphor implies not freedom but disorientation.
16: Lasim artsam leshamma lishreqat ˁolam — to make their land a desolation, a thing to be hissed at forever. Šerîqâ (hissing) is the onomatopoeic term for the sound passersby make when they see a devastated city — a universal cultural response to shocking destruction (cf. 1 Kings 9:8; Lam 2:15; Zeph 2:15). Kol-ˁover ˁaleyha yisham veyanoˁa berosho — everyone who passes will be appalled and shake his head: the head-shaking (nûaʿ bĕrōʾš) is the visual expression of stunned disbelief at what one sees.
17: Keruach qadim apitsem lifney oyev — like the east wind I will scatter them before the enemy. The east wind (rûaḥ qādîm) is the hamsin — the devastating hot wind that parches crops and destroys vegetation, an instrument of agricultural judgment (cf. Gen 41:6; Hos 13:15). ˁoref veloˁ-fanim erehˁem beyom ˁedam — my back and not my face I will show them in the day of their calamity. The contrast with the Aaronic blessing (yāʾēr YHWH pānāyw ʾēlêkā, Num 6:25) is total: the covenant face that shines on the blessed is replaced by the back of the departing God — the ultimate sign of abandoned covenant relationship.
18: Veyo-meru lechu venachsheva ˁal Yirmeyahu — then they said, Come, let us devise plans against Jeremiah. The conspiracy invokes institutional authority: ki lo-tovad Torah mikkohen veˁetsa mechakham vedavar minnevi — for instruction will not perish from the priest, counsel from the wise man, and word from the prophet. The three prophetic/wisdom streams (priest's tôrâ, sage's ʿēṣâ, prophet's dāḇār) are cited as proof that Jeremiah's radical message can be ignored — there are other authoritative voices. This is exactly the false-security thinking the book condemns throughout.
19: Hashiva YHWH elay ushmаˁ leqol yereivay — give attention to me, YHWH, and listen to the voice of those who contend with me. The fifth Confession begins (vv19-23). Jeremiah's switch from petition to imprecation follows the pattern of the individual lament psalms — from complaint to petition to trust, but in Jeremiah the resolution is darker. Rîḇ (contend/lawsuit) reappears — Jeremiah's adversaries are conducting a legal action against him.
20: Hayushalam tachat-tova raˁa ki karu shuchat lenafshi — is evil repaid for good? For they have dug a pit for my life. The rhetorical question inverts the lex talionis principle: Jeremiah interceded for the very people who now plot against him (kî-ˁāmadtî lĕpānêkā lĕdabbēr ʿălêhem ṭôḇ — I stood before you to speak good on their behalf). The intercession Jeremiah performed becomes the basis for his complaint that evil is returned for his good.
21: Lachen ten et-benehem laraˁav — therefore hand their children over to famine. Jeremiah's imprecatory prayer against his persecutors escalates through a comprehensive curse: famine for the children, sword for the men, plague for the adults, battle-death for the young men. The imprecation borrows the covenant curse vocabulary of Deuteronomy 28 — the very judgments Jeremiah has been announcing for the nation, he now invokes specifically on those who attacked him.
22: Tishmaˁ zeˁaqa mibteyhem behavi ˁaleyhem gedud pitem — let a cry be heard from their houses when you bring marauders against them suddenly. Gedûd (band of raiders, marauders) is the military unit that makes surprise attacks. Ki charu shuchat lelupdeni ulpachim tamnenu lereˁelay — for they have dug a pit to trap me and hidden snares for my feet: the trap-metaphors (pit and snare) are hunting imagery applied to the prophet's enemies.
23: Veatta YHWH yadaˁta et-kol-ˁatsatam ˁalay lamavet — but you, YHWH, know all their plots against my life. The imprecation ends with an appeal to YHWH as the omniscient judge (yādaʿtā, you know — the same yādaʿ of relational knowing as in 1:5). Al-tekapper ˁal-ˁavonam vehatatam milfanecha al-timcheh — do not forgive their guilt or blot out their sin from your sight. The reversal of the standard intercessory prayer (normally forgive the sin) is stark — Jeremiah explicitly asks YHWH not to forgive. This is the most morally disturbing of the Confessions and must be read as prophetic identification with the covenant's judgment-logic rather than personal vindictiveness.
